status: Run 2026-07-08 on one AWS r8a.4xlarge box. Memgraph benchgraph/Pokec (35 Cypher queries) through Fluree's Cypher surface over HTTP vs native Memgraph 3.11.0 (Bolt), Neo4j 5.26.28 Community (Bolt), and FalkorDB 4.18.11 (native Redis module, RESP). 35/35 pass on every engine at every scale; all four return byte-identical result sets (one FalkorDB filtered-path semantic caveat); all held to per-commit durability. Each engine measured on its real-world client transport (Fluree HTTP, Memgraph/Neo4j Bolt, FalkorDB RESP). Read geo mean (ms) — small Fluree 0.60 / Memgraph 0.93 / Neo4j 1.43 / FalkorDB 0.61; medium 1.23 / 2.36 / 4.99 / 1.75; large 1.47 / 4.41 / 6.80 / 4.57: Fluree fastest read engine at EVERY scale (1.6-3.0x vs Memgraph, 2.4-4.6x vs Neo4j, 1.0-3.1x vs FalkorDB; tied w/ FalkorDB at small, pulling away with scale). Write geo mean (durable): small 1.32 / 3.03 / 1.76 / 2.93; medium 1.27 / 3.39 / 2.94 / 3.36; large 1.73 / 4.46 / 4.07 / 4.57: Fluree wins durable writes 2.3-2.7x vs Memgraph/FalkorDB, 1.3-2.4x vs Neo4j at every scale. Division of strengths: Fluree wins the analytical half (whole-graph aggregates ~O(1) via index directories, 100-720x faster than scanners at large) + durable writes; FalkorDB keeps a NARROW traversal edge (fixed-hop expansion/neighbourhood ~1.2-1.5x on category geo, down from ~2-3x prior builds) but Fluree now overtakes on the deepest hops (expansion_3/expansion_4) at large; Memgraph is the balanced generalist + shortest-path leader at small/medium; Neo4j slowest. Numbers reflect the v4.1.2 build (13a78d2a) measured over HTTP; write path unchanged in this build. Fluree is the imminent v4.1.2 release.

dataset.name: Pokec (benchgraph / mgbench)
dataset.description: Slovak Pokec social network, Memgraph's published benchgraph distribution. Single :User node label, single Friend relationship type, no edge properties.
dataset.source: deps.memgraph.io (verbatim Memgraph distribution); queries = Neo4j-portable branch of memgraph/tests/mgbench/workloads/pokec.py

methodology.transport: Each engine measured over its real-world client transport, not normalised away: Fluree over its HTTP/JSON API (urllib + json.loads), Memgraph and Neo4j over Bolt (official neo4j driver), FalkorDB over native RESP (GRAPH.QUERY via falkordb client). Fluree's Bolt surface exists but neo4j-driver record deserialization dominates large-result latency; HTTP is the leaner, representative Fluree client and is what is reported here.
methodology.durability: all four engines fsync per commit (apples-to-apples writes): Memgraph --storage-wal-file-flush-every-n-tx=1, Neo4j durable by default, FalkorDB Redis AOF appendfsync always, Fluree per-commit on-disk index
methodology.clean_protocol: Reads on a pristine store: fresh load -> warmup (discard) -> reads-only passes -> separate write pass. Canonical reads = pristine 'reads2' pass; writes = 'full' pass. Critical for Fluree (reads on a store with accumulated write novelty inflate aggregate latency via novelty reconciliation).
methodology.shared_params_all_scales: All three scales measured for all four engines on one box with shared params_<scale>.json (fresh pristine load each). Result-set sizes matched byte-for-byte across all four engines (sole exception: FalkorDB shortest_path_with_filter semantic difference). Because expansion latency depends on start-node degree and the shared large params draw lower-degree roots than earlier-published sets, large traversal numbers are lower than previously published but apples-to-apples across engines.
methodology.correctness: At every scale all four engines returned identical result-set sizes for all 35 queries (one FalkorDB filtered-path caveat); the latency comparison is like-for-like work.
methodology.notes: Single-client isolated latency on current hardware; NOT comparable to Memgraph's published multi-worker throughput/mixed/realistic modes on older silicon. Absolute times are this-box-only.

fluree.label: Fluree
fluree.config: Source build (cargo build --release -p fluree-db-cli). Served over the HTTP/JSON API. FLUREE_CYPHER_ALLOW_FULL_SCAN=1 for whole-graph aggregate queries. Every measured query runs end-to-end through Fluree's Cypher surface (verbatim Neo4j query texts); SPARQL plays no part at query time.

load.method: native TTL bulk importer: fluree create pokec --from pokec_<scale>.ttl, then fluree context set pokec -e '{"@vocab":"http://example.org/"}' to bridge bare Cypher names to imported IRIs
load.notes: Load out of the measured path. Fresh pristine create per scale.

memgraph.label: Memgraph
memgraph.config: Bolt 7687, durable via --storage-wal-file-flush-every-n-tx=1 (per-commit fsync). Path queries in the published Memgraph suite use proprietary *bfs syntax; this run uses the Neo4j-portable query branch for a like-for-like comparison.

load.method: replay distribution Cypher CREATE statements (CREATE INDEX ON :User(id) first) -> CREATE SNAPSHOT -> restart in fsync mode
load.notes: Memgraph's PUBLISHED benchmark runs writes non-durably (WAL flush every 100k tx, sub-0.2 ms write floor); not comparable to a per-commit-durable engine and not measured here.

load.method: neo4j-admin database import full from generated CSVs (~19 s large vs hours for cypher-shell replay), then User(id) index + db.awaitIndexes()

falkordb.label: FalkorDB
falkordb.config: Native redis-server loading the falkordb.so module (NOT the Docker image), AOF appendfsync always on the host filesystem. Queries over native RESP GRAPH.QUERY via the falkordb python client (its experimental Bolt surface was not reliable enough to benchmark). Path queries use queries-falkordb/ overrides (shortestPath in WITH/RETURN; allShortestPaths in MATCH, hop bound ..2 to match reference).
falkordb.memory_footprint: large graph (30.6 M edges) = ~1286 MB resident (GraphBLAS compressed-sparse); whole working set in RAM, AOF/RDB for durability only, never serves queries from disk

load.method: native bulk loader falkordb-bulk-insert --nodes-with-label User --relations-with-type Friend --id-type INTEGER (CSVs generated from the .cypher via cypher_to_csv.py), then explicit CREATE INDEX FOR (u:User) ON (u.id) + plan verification
load.throughput: small 0.19 s; medium 3.1 s; large ~85 s (30.6 M edges)
load.notes: Two silent-failure modes worked around: default buffers/socket timeout drop edges past ~24 M (use a longer socket_timeout + larger buffers); --index does not always build on the 1.6 M-node graph (create explicitly and verify the plan uses an index scan).

caveats: Run 2026-07-08 on one AWS r8a.4xlarge; single-client isolated latency; each engine on its real-world client transport (Fluree HTTP, Memgraph/Neo4j Bolt, FalkorDB RESP).

Fluree is the fastest read engine at every scale on the blended geo-mean (tied with FalkorDB at small). Division of strengths: Fluree wins aggregates (~O(1) index directories, 100-720x vs scanners at large) + durable writes; FalkorDB keeps a narrow traversal edge (expansion/neighbourhood ~1.2-1.5x on category geo, down from ~2-3x) but Fluree overtakes on the deepest hops (expansion_3/expansion_4) at large; Memgraph balanced + shortest-path leader at small/medium; Neo4j slowest.
Writes are apples-to-apples only because all engines fsync per commit. Memgraph's published non-durable writes are faster but not comparable.

FalkorDB shortest_path_with_filter post-filters the shortest path rather than searching under the constraint (as Neo4j does), so it can return the empty path where Neo4j returns a longer constrained one - a semantic difference in 1 of 35 queries. FalkorDB point-to-point shortestPath is also its weakest category (~44-58 ms at large vs sub-2 ms for others).

Memory-bound scaling is NOT exercised on this 128 GB box: FalkorDB and Memgraph hold the whole graph in RAM (no disk paging; over-sized graph OOMs), Fluree is disk-backed and degrades gracefully. A smaller-machine run is the natural next step and the true architectural differentiator.
NOT comparable to Memgraph's published multi-worker throughput numbers (different mode, different hardware).
Fluree traversal optimization targets (shallow-expansion batching, pattern_long, unwind_range batch writes) are open; see FLUREE-GAPS.md.
